# Counting Exercise {auto-animate="true"}
<div style="position:absolute; top:50%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%, -50%); width:100%; display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; gap:0.2em; pointer-events:none;">
  <img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide" style="width:100%; display:block; margin:0 auto; ">
</div>
::: {.fragment}
<div style="position:absolute; top:45%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%, -50%); z-index:5; width:min(90%, 30em); sans-serif; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center; padding: 1em; border-radius: 10px; background-color: rgba(172, 172, 172, 0.95); font-weight: 700;">
Eighty-four (84) lives were saved by one AI-enabled warning system in one trial.^[[@rossettiRealtimeSurveillanceSystem2025]]
<!-- font-family: 'Optima'; Real-time surveillance system for patient deterioration: a pragmatic cluster-randomized controlled trial ^[[@rossettiRealtimeSurveillanceSystem2025]] -->
</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}

- 84 people, 84 lives are pictured on this slide.

- In a recent trial of an AI early warning system in New York City in two health systems, it was estimated that in one year, an estimated 84 people who might have died, didn’t.
- This is how important AI in healthcare can be: it has the potential to save lives by predicting and preventing adverse outcomes.
- This is the promise of machine learning in healthcare: to use data to predict and prevent adverse outcomes, and ultimately save lives.

- Across 60,893 hospital encounters, the intervention group experienced a 35.6% reduction in instantaneous in-hospital mortality risk (AHR  0.64; 95%CI 0.53–0.78; P < 0.0001) and an 11.2% shorter length of stay (IRR  0.91; 95%CI 0.90–0.93; P < 0.0001) compared to usual care.

:::
:::

<!-- 
## Digital Footprints of the Patient Journey

<div text-align="center">
![](figures/motivation/ehr_simplified.svg){fig-cap="EHR Data Structure" width=100%}
</div>
  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
       style="position:absolute; top:35%; left:0%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
<!-- 
  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
       style="position:absolute; top:30%; left:55%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div> -->


::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Patients generate digital footprints of their health journey when they visit care providers.
- These footprints are often stored in electronic health records (EHRs), which contain a wealth of information about patients' medical history, diagnoses, treatments, and outcomes.
- It's a complex, heterogeneous, and often messy collection of structured codes (diagnoses, labs, medications) and unstructured text (clinical notes).
   
- This complexity is both a challenge and an opportunity for machine learning in healthcare. 

- The richness of EHR data allows for powerful predictive models, but also requires careful handling to ensure reproducibility and clinical relevance.
:::
::: 


# ML/AI for Health in a Nutshell {data-section-title="ML for Health in a Nutshell ⚕️"}
<!-- ![](figures/motivation/ml_for_health.svg){fig-cap="World Health Organization" height=25em .no-dark} -->
<div style="position: relative; display: inline-block;">
  <img src="figures/motivation/ml_for_health.svg" style="height:25em; display:block;" class="no-dark">

  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
       style="position:absolute; top:0%; left:15%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>

  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
       style="position:absolute; top:0; left:54%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- So let's first look at the basics of machine learning in healthcare.
- Hospitals, clinics, and healthcare systems generate vast amounts of data, including electronic health records (EHRs), medical imaging, and physiological signals.
- Machine learning can be used on parts of this data to predict patient outcomes, assist in diagnosis, and optimize treatment plans.
- The essence is often predicting or diagnosing something about a patient at time T so that we can intervene at time T+1 to improve outcomes.
:::
:::


## The Promise and Reality of AI in Healthcare
ArXiv papers with **AI and health keywords** in title/abstract and FDA-approved **AI medical devices** ^[[@healthArtificialIntelligenceEnabledMedicalTue03/10/2026-09:05]]:
<div style="margin-top: -1.2em;">
![](figures/motivation/ml_health_trend_fda.svg){fig-cap="EHR Data Structure" width=100%}
</div>
<div style="position: relative; top: -1.9em;">
``` code
ML_PATTERN = r"(?ix)\b(machine[ _]?learning|deep[ _]?learning|neural[ _]?networks?|artificial[ _]?intelligence|\bAI\b|random[ _]?forest|support[ _]?vector[ _]?(machine|regression|classif\w+)?|\bSVM\b|gradient[ _]?boost\w*|\bXGBoost\b|\bLightGBM\b|\bCatBoost\b|\btransformer\b|\bBERT\b|\bGPT\b|\bLLM\b|\bVLM\b|large[ _]?language[ _]?models?|foundation[ _]?models?|(convolutional|recurrent|graph)[ _]?neural|\bLSTM\b|\bGRU\b|generative[ _]?adversarial|\bGAN\b|reinforcement[ _]?learning|(semi|self|un)[ _-]?supervised[ _]?learning|transfer[ _]?learning|federated[ _]?learning|natural[ _]?language[ _]?processing|\bNLP\b|computer[ _]?vision|(image|object|text)[ _]?(classification|detection|segmentation)|\bautoencoder\b|\bdiffusion[ _]?model\b)\b"
HEALTH_PATTERN = r"(?ix)\b(health(care)?|medical|clinical|patients?|hospital|diseases?|disorders?|syndrome|diagnosis|diagnostics?|prognosis|prognostic|treatments?|therapy|therapeutic|\bEHR\b|\bEMR\b|epidemiol\w*|radiol\w*|pathol\w*|genomics?|proteomics?|transcriptomics?|biomarkers?|\bdrug\b|pharmaceutical|pharmacolog\w*|cancer|tumor|tumour|oncol\w*|cardiac|cardiovascular|coronary|diabetes|diabetic|\bCOVID\b|\bSARS\b|pandemic|epidemic|biomedical|mortality|morbidity|\bICU\b|intensive[ _]?care|surgical|surgery|symptoms?|comorbid\w*|screening|triage|mental[ _]?health|psychiatr\w*|neurolog\w*|\bMRI\b|\bCT[ _]?scan\b)\b"

```
</div>

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}

- The amount of research on machine learning in health has been growing rapidly, reflecting the increasing interest and potential of this field as shown here from the ArXiv preprint server with ML and health keywords in the title or abstract. Total amount of papers: 3,008,410.
- However, despite this growth in research, the translation of ML4H research into clinical practice is limited as can be seen from the relatively small number of FDA-approved AI/ML-enabled medical devices. 
- 76% of the 331 FDA devices approved in 2025 is in radiology, which is has a long history of using machine learning for image analysis, but other areas of healthcare are still underrepresented in terms of AI-enabled devices.
:::
:::
<!-- > "Despite significant advances in machine learning capabilities, few AI systems have been successfully deployed into clinical practice. Key barriers include reproducibility challenges, generalization failures, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks." -->

<!-- :::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Current State:**
- Limited open-source tools
- Dataset-specific implementations
- Proprietary data formats
- Few external validations
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Consequences:**
- Non-reproducible results
- Slow scientific progress
- Inefficient use of clinical data
- Barriers to adoption
:::
:::: -->

# The Translation Gap in Healthcare {data-section-title="The Translation Gap in Healthcare 🏥" auto-animate="true"}


  
<!-- - <span style="color: #0095D1">Advance the implementation of national digital health strategies</span> -->
::: {.callout-warning style="font-size: 1.5em;" appearance="simple" }
**Few AI/ML models have been successfully deployed in clinical practice.**^[[@apellExplainingSlowAdoption2026] [@henzlerHealthcareProfessionalsPerspectives2025]]
:::

Main barriers to translation:


::: {.fragment .fade-in}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/eye.svg){height=2em .light-only}

⚡ <span data-id="problem-incomplete-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p1_label >}}</span>^[[@agnielBiasesElectronicHealth2018]]
:::
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/repeat.svg){height=2em .light-only}

⚡ <span data-id="problem-repro-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p2_label >}}</span>^[[@futomaMythGeneralisabilityClinical2020]]
:::
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/infrastructure.svg){height=2em .light-only}

⚡ <span data-id="problem-infrastructure-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p3_label >}}</span>^[[@mcdermottLackScienceMachine2025; @donohoDataScienceSingularity2024]]
:::
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in}
*Out of scope: ​⚡​ ** {{< var p4_label >}}**^[[@vancalsterThereNoSuch2023]] and ​⚡​**$P_5$ Financial incentives**^[[@abramoffScalingAdoptionMedical2024]] *
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- There is something of a translation gap in ML in healthcare: despite the promise of machine learning research, new datasets, and advances in methodology few models have been successfully deployed in clinical practice.
- **{{< var p1_label >}}**: Retrospective data may not capture the real-time information that is needed for prediction.
- **{{< var p2_label >}}**: Different papers report different performance for the same task due to variations in datasets, preprocessing, and evaluation.
- **{{< var p3_label >}}**: Each institution has to build their health AI from scratch, making it hard to share research results and build on each other's work.
- **{{< var p4_label >}}**: I will not go into detail today.
:::
:::

## Research Contribution per Challenge {auto-animate=true}


⚡ <span data-id="problem-incomplete-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p1_label >}}</span>  <span class="fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="1" data-id="incomplete-phrase">→ {{< var r1_label >}}</span>

⚡ <span data-id="problem-repro-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p2_label >}}</span> <span class="fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="2" data-id="repro-phrase">→ {{< var r2_label >}}</span>

⚡ <span data-id="problem-infrastructure-shared" style="display:inline-block; font-weight:700;">{{< var p3_label >}}</span>  <span class="fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index="3" data-id="infrastructure-phrase">→ {{< var r3_label >}}</span>

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
{{< include includes/statement.qmd >}}
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}

::: {.slide-card}
::: {.column width="80%"}
UN WHO Global Strategy on Digital Health Objectives (1/4):

*"To promote digital health collaborations and
partnership models within and across organizations on the use of software global goods, open-standards, and common digital health
architecture."* ^[[@marianoGlobalStrategyDigital2020]]
:::
::: {.column width="20%"}

![](figures/motivation/WHO_logo.svg){fig-cap="World Health Organization" height=5em .no-dark fig-align="right"}
:::
:::
:::


::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
My thesis is structured around three main contributions:

-  We conducted a prospective cohort study, which collects data from surgical patients in the general ward and integrates wearable device data to improve early warning systems for postoperative complications.
- We developed the YAIB and Recipies framework to lower the barrier for reproducible research and enable better validation of ML for health studies.
- I contributed to the development of the MEDS framework, which provides a standardized data format and tools for scalable machine learning research across healthcare institutions.
- Thesis Statement: The overarching thesis is that healthcare machine learning requires rich data, reusable model components, and an extensive validation ecosystem to bridge the gap between research and clinical practice.
- This aligns well with the WHO's global strategy on digital health, which emphasizes the importance of open standards and collaboration in this field.
:::
:::

## Research Contributions in Pillars {auto-animate=true auto-animate-id="pub-flow"}

:::: columns

<span data-id="incomplete-phrase">{{< var r1_label >}}</span>

Sauer, and Max M. Maurer. Continuous Multimodal AI with Wearable Vital Signs Predicts Postoperative Complications in the General Ward. Nov. 2025. doi: 10.1101/2025.11.25.25340950. :::

"Combining Hospital-grade Clinical Data and Wearable Vital Sign Monitoring to Predict Surgical Complications". Mar. 2024. [@waterCombiningHospitalgradeClinical2024b]
:::

**Robin van de Water**, Axel Winter, Max M. Maurer, Felix August Treykorn, Bjarne Pfitzner, Igor M. Sauer, and Bert Arnrich. "Combining Time Series Modalities to Create Endpoint-driven Patient Records". [@waterCombiningTimeSeries2024]
:::
:::
:::

<span data-id="repro-phrase">{{< var r2_label >}}</span>

**Robin P. van de Water**, Hendrik Schmidt, and Patrick Rockenschaub. "ReciPies: A Lightweight Data Transformation Pipeline for Reproducible ML"., p. 9261. doi: 10.21105/joss.09261 [@van_de_Water2026]
:::
::: {.pub-box data-id="pub-r2-yaib"}
**Robin van de Water**, Hendrik Nils Aurel Schmidt, Paul Elbers, Patrick Thoral, Bert Arnrich, and Patrick Rockenschaub. "Yet Another ICU Benchmark: A Flexible Multi-Center Framework for Clinical ML". doi: 10.48550/arXiv.2306.05109. [@vandewaterAnotherICUBenchmark2024a]
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
<span data-id="infrastructure-phrase">{{< var r3_label >}}</span>

Matthew McDermott, Ethan Steinberg, Jason Fries, **Robin P. van de Water**, Chao Pang, Patrick Rockenschaub, Pawel Renc, Jungwoo Oh, Kamilė Stankevičiūtė, Justin Xu, Tom Joseph Pollard, Nassim Oufattole, Michael Wornow, Teya Bergamaschi, Hyewon Jeong, Simon Lee, Vincent Jeanselme, Kiril Klein, Mikkel Odgaard, Maria Elkjær Montgomery, Arkadiusz Sitek, Mads Nielsen, Jeffrey Chiang, Noa Dagan, Isaac Kohane, Shalmali Joshi, Edward Choi, and Nigam Shah. [@McDermott2025MEDS]
:::

:::
::: {.pub-box data-id="pub-r3-meds-dev"}
Matthew B. A. McDermott, Aleksia Kolo, Chao Pang, Edward Choi, Ethan Steinberg, Hyewon Jeong, Jack Gallifant, Jason Alan Fries, Jeffrey N. Chiang, Jungwoo Oh, Justin Xu, Kamilė Stankevičiūtė, Kiril Vadimovic Klein, Mikkel Fruelund Odgaard, Nassim Oufattole, Patrick Rockenschaub, Pawel Renc, **Robin van de Water**, Shalmali Joshi, Simon Austin Lee, Teya Bergamaschi, Tom Pollard, Vincent Jeanselme, Nigam Shah, Michael Wornow, Aparajita Kashyap, Xinzhou Jiang, Yanwei Li, Young Sang Choi, Yuta Kobayashi, and Ryan King. [@mcdermottMEDSDecentralizedExtensible2024]
:::
::: {.pub-box data-id="pub-r3-meds-kdd"}
Matthew B. A. McDermott, Justin Xu, Teya S. Bergamaschi, Hyewon Jeong, Simon A. Lee, Nassim Oufattole, Patrick Rockenschaub, Kamilė Stankevičiūtė, Ethan Steinberg, Jimeng Sun, **Robin P. Van De Water**, Michael Wornow, John Wu, and Zhenbang Wu. "MEDS: Building Models and Tools in a Reproducible Health AI Ecosystem". SIGKDD Tutorial [@mcdermottMEDSBuildingModels2025a]
:::
::: {.pub-box data-id="pub-r3-scaling-talk"}
**Robin P. Van de Water**. "Scaling Up Clinical ML from Datasets to Entire Health Systems through the MEDS Ecosystem". In: Berlin, June 2025 [@vandewaterScalingClinicalML2025]
:::
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Here you can find the publications that correspond to each of the three main contributions I will talk about today organized by research pillar.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}

:::

## {data-section-title="{{< var section_t1 >}}"}
<div style="position:absolute; inset:0; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; text-align:center; font-size:2.2em; font-weight:700;">
  ⚡ {{< var p1_label >}}
</div>

# Multi-ward Multi-modal Early Warning Systems 🛏️ {data-section-title="{{< var section_t1 >}}"}
**​⚡​ {{< var p1_label >}}**: Retrospective, observational, data does not accurately capture the state of the patient.


::: {.fragment .fade-in .card-row .card-row--stack fragment-index="1"}
![](icons/surgeon.svg){fig-cap="" height=2em .no-dark}

**300M+** surgeries annually^[[@dobsonTraumaMajorSurgery2020]].
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in .card-row .card-row--stack fragment-index="2" }
![](icons/hospital.svg){fig-cap="" height=2em .no-dark}
    
**~1/3 of hospital costs** are surgery-related^[[@shrimeGlobalBurdenSurgical2015]].
:::

::: {.fragment .card-row .card-row--stack .fade-in fragment-index="3"}
![](icons/alert.svg){fig-cap="" height=2em .no-dark}

**Up to 25%** experience life-threatening adverse events, often **on the ward**^[[@downeyImpactInhospitalPostoperative2023; @muallaImpactPostoperativeComplications2021; @baumMortalityComplicationsFollowing2019a]].
:::

::: {.fragment .card-row .card-row--stack .fade-in fragment-index="4"}
![](icons/heartbeat.svg){fig-cap="" height=2em .no-dark}
    
Ward monitoring insufficient for **subtle condition changes**^[[@roppeltArtificialIntelligenceHealthcare2024;@stamPredictionSurgicalComplications2022]].
:::


::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- The first contribution is related to the limitation of retrospective data in capturing real-time state information. This leads to models that don't capture the full complexity of the patient journey. This is reflected in the case of surgery rehabilitation.
- Surgery is a major part of healthcare, with more than 300 million surgical procedures performed annually worldwide
- Surgery is also a major driver of healthcare costs, with approximately one-third of hospital costs being surgery-related.
- Postoperative complications are common, with up to 25% of patients experiencing adverse events after surgery which can lead to death and significantly impact quality of life.
- But, preceding physiological patterns are often subtle, nonspecific, and time-varying, with substantial heterogeneity between patients → hard to detect on ward, where most postoperative patients rehabilitate.
- Why not just monitor patients in the ICU? → expensive and can lead to complications like infections, delirium, and deconditioning.

:::
:::

<!-- ## Surgery Rehabilitation
::: {.incremental}
- More than 300 million surgical procedures performed annually worldwide^[[@dobsonTraumaMajorSurgery2020]]
- 1/3 of hospital costs are surgery-related^[[@shrimeGlobalBurdenSurgical2015]]
- Postoperative adverse events impact consequent quality of life, healthcare costs, and mortality^[[@brownImpactPostoperativeComplications2014; @downeyImpactInhospitalPostoperative2023; @kongASOVisualAbstract2021; @muallaImpactPostoperativeComplications2021; @sartelliComplicatedIntraabdominalInfections2014; @vonlanthenImpactComplicationsCosts2011; @wangPostoperativeComplicationsPrognosis2019]]
- Why not just monitor patients in the ICU?
  - ICU stays are expensive and can lead to complications like infections, delirium, and deconditioning.
- Solution? Hybrid ward with minimally intrusive wearables
::: -->

<!-- 
## Why not just monitor patients in the ICU?

::: {.incremental}
- ICU AI model research is booming^[[@hylandEarlyPredictionCirculatory2020a; @nematiInterpretableMachineLearning2018]], but:
  - ICU costs are high, recovery slow(er)
- Nursing ward is less resource constrained, but:
  - Severely less monitored
- Solution? Hybrid ward with minimally intrusive wearables
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- So why not just monitor patients in the ICU?
- ICU stays are expensive and can lead to complications like infections, delirium, and deconditioning.
:::
:::

--- -->

## A Prospective Cohort Study: "Hybrid Ward" {auto-animate="true"}
::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;"}
Can we leverage **continuous data from wearable devices** to improve warning systems for postoperative complications (surgical site infections) in the general ward? ^[[@vandewaterCombiningHospitalgradeClinical2024; @waterCombiningHospitalgradeClinical2024b]{.my-work}]
:::
<div style="position: relative; top: -1.1em;">
::: {.fragment .fade-in}
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figures/cass/wards.png){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
<div data-id="recipies-logo-box" style="position:absolute; right:85%; top:30%; width:12%; text-align:right;">
  <img src="images/logos/logo_experimental_surgery.png" style="width:100%;" class="no-dark">
  <img src="images/logos/Logo_Charite.svg" style="width:100%;" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
<div style="display:flex; gap:0.35em; align-items:flex-start;">
  <div style="flex:1;">
![](figures/cass/corsano.png){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
  </div>
  <div style="flex:1;">
![](figures/cass/greenteg_core.jpg){fig-cap="" width=75% .no-dark}
  </div>
</div>
<div style="position:relative; top:-2em; text-align:center;">
![](icons/bluetooth_mesh.svg){fig-cap="" width=50% .no-dark}
</div>
:::
<!-- <div style="position:absolute; top:50%; left:45%; width:30%; height:35%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div> -->
:::
</div>
::: {.fragment .fade-in }
<div style="position: relative; top: -5em;">
![](figures/cass/cass_study_curation.svg){fig-cap="From burden to monitoring gap" width=100% .no-dark}
</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- What could be a solution? **Hybrid ward with minimally intrusive wearables**
- With the experimental surgery group at Charite, we conducted a prospective cohort study in a hybrid ward setting, where regular care was provided and continuous, data from wearable devices was collected through informed consent.
- Corsano band: Photoplethysmogram (PPG) Sensor (HR/BP), Acceleration, oxygen saturation, Respiration Rate
- Temperature sensor patch for core body temperature
- Feed data into a Bluetooth mesh network, which allows for real-time monitoring and data collection without the need for constant manual intervention.
- Left: Timeline of the perioperative patient journey in surgical patients. 
- This is largest reported cohort to investigate continuous, telemetric vital sign analysis in the field of surgical care.
- Right: Prospective data curation by endpoint labeling and data extraction from hospital data information systems.
:::
:::


## {auto-animate="true"}
<!-- ## Introducing Clinical ASsist Heuristic Early Warning System (CASHEWS) -->
<!-- <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
style="position:absolute; top:10%; left:0%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
style="position:absolute; top:71%; left:0%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -2.2em;">
  <img src="figures/cass/cass_pipeline.svg" width="60%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->
<!-- <div style="position: relative; top: -2em;">
![](figures/cass/cass_study_curation.svg){fig-cap="From burden to monitoring gap" width=100%}
</div> -->
<div style="position: relative; text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
![](figures/cass/cass_pipeline_multimodal.svg){fig-cap="" width=90% .no-dark}^[[@vandewaterContinuousMultimodalAI2025] {.my-work}]
</div>






::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- This figure shows our end-to-end multimodal prediction pipeline, from raw data to dynamic risk predictions.
- We start with multiple data sources: wearable and physiologic signals, body temperature, medications, clinical notes, labs, ICU vitals and observations, plus baseline patient characteristics.
- Each modality is preprocessed with methods matched to its type, including MiniRocket embeddings, HRV feature extraction, multilingual text embeddings, and clinical harmonization and aggregation.
- Next, we apply temporal processing: imputation, statistical feature extraction, and retrospective windows from 24 hours down to 1 hour before the prediction time.
- These modality-specific representations are fused into one multimodal model input.
- The model then predicts endpoint risk over multiple horizons, from 12 hours to 0 hours, updating dynamically every 30 minutes. In total, this produces 7,280 features over 371,813 segmented trajectories.


 <!-- - Schematic overview of data extraction, domain-specific preprocessing, and temporal aggregation. Structured clinical data comprising static, categorical, and binary variables (e.g., demographic data, comorbidities, preoperative laboratory values) and dynamic time-varying measurements (e.g., repeated laboratory examinations, intensive care unit (ICU) vital signs) were processed using domain-specific preprocessing pipelines. 
 - Unstructured clinical text data, including clinical notes, medication orders, and fluid administration records, were embedded using a pre-trained multilingual JinAI model²⁷, from which standard natural language processing (NLP) features were derived. High-resolution physiological signals derived from patient wearables on the general ward, specifically photoplethysmography (PPG) and continuous body temperature measurement, were processed using the MINIROCKET embedding model. 
 - Heart rate variability (HRV) features were extracted from both time- and frequency-domain representations. Missing values were imputed, and statistical descriptors—including skewness, entropy, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum—were computed. To capture temporal dependencies, features from preceding time steps were incorporated. 
 - All data streams were aggregated into 30-minute time segments, each containing 7,280 distinct model features. Multi-modal feature fusion and endpoint alignment resulted in segmented clinical trajectories evaluated at a 9-hour prediction horizon.  -->
:::
:::

---

## Clinical ASsist Heuristic Early Warning System+

- Use open-source framework for **training and model selection**.
<div style="position: relative; text-align: center; margin-top: 2.5em;">
![](figures/cass/cass_model_training.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark}
</div>
<img src="figures/cass/cashews_logo.svg" width=100% class="no-dark" style="position:absolute; top:53%; left:85%; width:13%; z-index:1;">
<img src="figures/yaib/yaib_logo.svg" width=100% class="no-dark" style="position:absolute; top:57%; left:35%; width:15%; z-index:1;">

 <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1" style="position:absolute; top:20%; left:52%; width:100%; height:55%; background:white; z-index:1;"></div> 

<div style="position: relative; top: 3em;">

::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="1"}
- Use optimal model outputs with **alarm mechanics** to reduce **alarm burden (alarms/patient/day)** while maintaining precision.^[Work with master thesis student Daniela Zuluaga Lotero].
- **Decision curve analysis:** harm-to-benefit ratio drives alarm selection.  
:::
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Multiple model architectures, encompassing classical machine learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL), were trained and evaluated using cross-validation and hyperparameter optimization. 
- Model selection was based on predictive performance and computational efficiency, with LightGBM identified as the most favorable trade-off. 
- The optimal model was evaluated by performing domain ablation and feature importance analysis with Shapley explanations. 
- Based upon continuous model outputs, an actional alarm system including specific alarming frameworks was evaluated. The slope coefficient and persistence quantify the increase in the probability of sounding an alarm. 
- An 8-hour silencing period is implemented to prevent alarm fatigue.
:::
:::

## How well does it work?
<!-- Clinical ASsist Heuristic Early Warning System (CASHEWS) -->

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div style="position: relative; top: -1em;">

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -0em;">
  <img src="figures/cass/performance_progression.svg" width="80%" class="no-dark">
</div>
</div>
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2" style="position:absolute; top:5%; left:25%; width:100%; height:70%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>

:::
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment fragment-index="3"}
<div style="position: relative; top: -1em;">

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -0em;">
  <img src="figures/cass/horizon_results.svg" width="80%" class="no-dark">
</div>
</div>
:::
:::

<div style="position: relative; top: -2.5em;">
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="2"}
<!-- - Dynamic baseline: REWS^[[@vanderstamWearablePatchBased2023]]. -->
→ CASHEWS(+) significantly improves **discrimination performanc**e.
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="3"}
→ **Lead time** performance has little drop-off.
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index="4"}
→  Monitoring **at the right time** extends AI-assisted surveillance to resource-constrained **wards**.
:::

</div>


::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Metrics: areas under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) and (b) boxplots of patient-level areas under the precision–recall curve (AUPRC) 
- First: the static baseline model that aggregate the data until the start of the surgery, then directly after the surgery and directly after the ICU stay. Static models using only preoperative and ICU data performed near chance level.
- Then the dynamic, longitudinal model that aggregates the data in 30-minute time windows. Where we have CASHEWS without wearable data and CASHEWS+ with wearable data. These models had much more predictive power for identifying surgical site infections.
- We also compare this with a baseline of the Remote Early Warning Score (REWS), which is a threshold-based wearable score.
- Looking at the lead time performance, we see that there is little drop-off in performance as we move further away from the clinical detection of the complication, which suggests that there are indicators of deterioration that can be detected well in advance of clinical recognition.
- Boxplots of AUROC and (d) boxplots of AUPRC of CASHEWS+ for varying lead times prior to clinical detection. The last hour was set to be positive, while all subsequent time windows were removed from training and evaluation.
- **The key implication is that due to the filling this missing data gap, we can do predictive surveillance can be extended beyond the ICU into general wards, where many actionable postoperative complications actually emerge.**
:::
:::



<!--

<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -2em;">
  <img src="figures/cass/fig3.svg" width="75%" class="no-dark">
</div>

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
(a) Boxplots of areas under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and (b) boxplots of patient-level areas under the precision–recall curve (AUPRC) for prediction performance of models after ablation of individual modalities. (c) AUROC and (d) AUPRC boxplots after addition of wearable-derived signal features (e) Receiver operating curve and (f) precision-recall curves comparing CASHEWS, CASHEWS+, and the remote early warning score (REWS). 
:::
:::
-->

<!-- <div style="position:absolute; top:0%; left:0%; width:100%; height:60%; background:white; z-index:0;"></div> -->




<!-- ## Performance Analysis

::: {.incremental}

- The best setup combined manufacturer-derived features with raw PPG embeddings, suggesting that physiological deterioration is better captured through dynamic complexity than through simple threshold-based monitoring.
- Important contributors included heart rate variability, temperature entropy, and respiratory rate, which are consistent with early autonomic stress, loss of physiological complexity, and impending decompensation.
- Gradient Boosted Trees outperformed deep learning in this setting.
- Future semi-autonomous surgical ward with continuous telemonitoring and AI-assisted risk prioritization.
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- The strongest gains came from continuous, high-resolution wearable data, nearly doubling AUPRC versus models without sensor integration.
::: -->

<!-- ## Clinical use, limitations, next steps

::: {.incremental}

- Future semi-autonomous surgical ward with continuous telemonitoring and AI-assisted risk prioritization.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}

::: -->


## {data-section-title="{{< var section_t2 >}}"}
<div style="position:absolute; inset:0; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; text-align:center; font-size:2.2em; font-weight:700;">
  ⚡ {{< var p2_label >}}
</div>

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- **Now that we've looked at a specific clinical use case of ML lets see how easy it is for others can reproduce these results and build on them.**
:::
:::
:::

# Enabling Reproducible Prediction Experiments 🧪 {data-section-title="{{< var section_t2 >}}"}

::: {.fragment}
​⚡ **{{< var p2_label >}}**^[[@moorEarlyPredictionSepsis2021;@fleurenMachineLearningPrediction2020a]]:​ Comparing approaches across papers is difficult because of:
:::



::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="20%" .fragment .centering}
![](icons/dataset.svg){fig-align="center" .pillar-icon}

<div style="text-align: center;">
Data/Splits
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="20%" .fragment}
![](icons/people.svg){fig-align="center" .pillar-icon}

<div style="text-align: center;">
Cohort
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="20%" .fragment}
![](icons/cogs.svg){fig-align="center" .pillar-icon}

<div style="text-align: center;">
Preprocessing
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="20%" .fragment}
![](icons/measure.svg){fig-align="center" .pillar-icon}

<div style="text-align: center; width:100%;">
Evaluation
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="20%" .fragment}
![](icons/code.svg){fig-align="center" .pillar-icon}

<div style="text-align: center;">
Code/Models^[[@shillanUseMachineLearning2019a]]
</div>
:::
:::


<!-- ::: 
::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;"}
Individual research results (models, techniques, ideas) do not generalize accross settings, hindering meaningful scientific progress and clinical translation. ^[[@moorEarlyPredictionSepsis2021;@fleurenMachineLearningPrediction2020a]] - *Dataset, cohort definition, and preprocessing choices can have **larger effects on performance** than the choice of model architecture.* 
::: -->
::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;" .fragment}
Can we create a framework for **reliably reproducing scientific results** of clinical prediction models across datasets and preprocessing choices?
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- That leads me to the second contribution, which is about enabling reproducible research in  clinical machine learning.
- The reproducibility crisis in clinical machine learning is a significant barrier to scientific progress and clinical translation.
-  **Datasets** (private datasets or different public datasets) and splits
-  **Cohort definitions** (patient inclusion/exclusion varies)
-  **Preprocessing** (normalization, imputation, features)
-  **Evaluation metrics**
- **Code not published, incomplete, not reusable or adaptable.** 
:::
:::

## Considerations for Reproducible Clinical ML Research

::: columns
::: {.column width="75%"}
Problems:

- Medical research is **inherently** complex.
- One size fits-all always **impossible**.
- Hardcoded ≠ **reproducible**.

::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="2"}
Goals:

- **Modular** setup maintaining reproducibility.
- **Extensibility** across experiment lifecycle.
- **Out-of-the-box** support: datasets, tasks, models.
:::
::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
Outcome:

- **ReciPies**^[[@van_de_Water2026]{.my-work}]: Declare, execute, and share ML preprocessing pipelines.
- **Yet Another ICU Benchmark (YAIB)**^[[@vandewaterAnotherICUBenchmark2024a]{.my-work}]: Reproducible prediction experiments across (open-access) datasets.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="3"}
![](figures/yaib/recipies_logo.svg){fig-cap="ReciPies Logo" width=100%}
![](figures/yaib/YAIB_logo.svg){fig-cap="YAIB Logo" width=100%}
:::
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- So what are the considerations that we need to take into account when designing a framework for reproducible clinical machine learning research?
- First, we need to acknowledge that medical research is inherently complex, and that one size fits all solutions are often impossible. This means that we need to design a framework that is modular and extensible, allowing researchers to adapt it to their specific needs while maintaining reproducibility.
- Second, we need to ensure that the framework provides customization and extension and that there is out-of-the-box support for common datasets, tasks, and models.
- To address these considerations, we developed two tools: ReciPies, a modular ML preprocessing framework, that allows you to declare preprocessing steps
- Yet Another ICU Benchmark (YAIB), a framework for reproducible ICU prediction experiments across 5 existing datasets and 5 common tasks.
- Open-access Intensive Care Unit (ICU) data has become popular for applications, models, and benchmarks in ML for health, but comparing approaches often remains difficult
:::
:::

<!-- Modular ML preprocessing framework -->
 <!-- enables reproducible comparisons while remaining flexible enough to accommodate real-world research needs.  -->
<!-- ::: {.incremental}
1. **Data Harmonization** [@Ben23]
2. **Modular Preprocessing** (ReciPies) [@vSR26]
3. **Standardized, explicit, configurable evaluation** [@DGS18]
::: -->

## Different Definitions, Different Conclusions
**Sepsis**: Life-threatening **response to infection** that causes injury to its own tissues and organs.

→ ⚡ Problem: no single, universally accepted **definition**.
<!-- | **Algorithm** | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Seymour et al.^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016]]*} | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Moor et al. ^[[@moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021]]} | \multicolumn{2}{c}{Calvert et al. ^[[@calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]]} -->


::: {.fragment data-fragment-index="3"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="100%"}
**Prediction** on MIMIC-IV^[[@johnsonMIMICIVFreelyAccessible2023]] using Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM) models with **different cohort definitions** from literature^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016; @moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021; @calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]]:

<div style="text-align: center;">
![](figures/yaib/yaib_sepsis_AUROC_grouped.svg){caption="Sepsis Definitions auroc" width=50% .no-dark}
</div>
<div data-id="recipies-logo-box" style="position:absolute; right:2%; top:80%; width:12%; text-align:right;">
  <img src="figures/yaib/yaib_logo.svg" style="width:100%;" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::
<!-- ::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/yaib/yaib_sepsis_AUPRC_grouped.svg){caption="Sepsis definition auprc" width=100% .no-dark}
::: -->
:::
<!-- | **Algorithm** | Seymour et al.^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016]]* |         | Moor et al.^[[@moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021]] |         | Calvert et al.^[[@calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]] |         |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|   | **AUROC** | **AUPRC** | **AUROC** | **AUPRC** | **AUROC** | **AUPRC** |
| **LGBM** | 75.9±0.2 | 4.3±0.0 | 72.4±0.0 | 10.5±0.0 | 62.2±0.2 | 1.8±0.0 |
| **GRU** | **79.2±0.1** | **6.1±0.0** | **80.9±0.0** | **17.7±0.0** | **89.2±0.0** | **9.3±0.2** | -->

<!-- - Different sepsis definitions^[[@seymourAssessmentClinicalCriteria2016; @moorPredictingSepsisMultisite2021; @calvertComputationalApproachEarly2016]] can significantly alter the resulting Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) -->
<!-- - Different definitions capture different patient populations, leading to varying model performance and clinical implications. -->

:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- To demonstrate the need of a common reference point for reproducible research, we can look at the task of sepsis prediction, which is a common task in clinical machine learning.
- Sepsis is a body's extreme response to an infection, which can lead to tissue damage, organ failure, and death. 
- However, there are different definitions of sepsis that have been proposed in the literature, and these definitions can significantly alter the resulting performance metrics of models trained to predict sepsis.
- This then leads to varying model performance and clinical implications.
- For clinicians this means that selecting the right model for their setting is difficult without a common reference point.
- This underlines the need for a framework that can test the effect of different definitions, preprocessing choices, and datasets on model performance in a reproducible way.

:::
:::
---

## YAIB: Mortality Prediction Across Datasets

::: {.column width="50%" }
**External validation:** training on row dataset, evaluation on column dataset using GRU:
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1em;">
![](figures/yaib/ext_val_mortality_pooled_auroc.svg){fig-cap="External Validation - Mortality AUROC" width=85% .no-dark}
</div>
<details>
<summary></summary>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -2em;">
<img src="figures/yaib/ext_val_mortality_pooled_auprc.svg" width="85%" class="no-dark">
</div>
</details>
:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment }
**Transfer learning:** Train a model on "generalizable" eICU and **finetune** on HiRID:
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1em;">
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning.svg){fig-cap="Transfer Learning - Mortality AUROC" width=76% .no-dark}
</div>
<details>
  <summary></summary>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -2em;">
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning_auprc.svg){fig-cap="Transfer Learning - Mortality AUPRC" width=76% .no-dark}
</div>
</details>

:::
<div data-id="recipies-logo-box" style="position:absolute; right:2%; top:80%; width:12%; text-align:right;">
  <img src="figures/yaib/yaib_logo.svg" style="width:100%;" class="no-dark">
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Using YAIB, we can easily compare generalization across datasets and transfer learning approaches for mortality prediction. Each row corresponds to a model trained on a dataset, and the columns correspond to the evaluation performance.
- Some datasets are more similar to others. The European datasets are different from the US datasets. However, eICU, which consists of data from 208 US hospitals is more generalizable than the other datasets, which are from single hospitals.
- Finetuning on the "difficult" HiRID dataset can improve performance with only a fraction of the data, suggesting that pretraining on a large, generalizable dataset can help overcome some of the challenges of dataset shift and improve generalization to new settings.
- **The key point is that without a framework like YAIB, it would be difficult to enable this important reproducible research across datasets.**
:::
:::

<!-- ::: {.fragment}

- **Column**: Model trained on dataset
- **Row**: Model evaluated on dataset
- **Diagonal**: Internal validation (same dataset)

::: -->


<!-- ## Transfer Learning

- What if we train a generalized model on "most generalizable" eICU?
- Finetune on "difficult" HiRID

::: {.fragment}

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUROC:
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning.svg){fig-cap="Transfer Learning - Mortality AUROC" width=100% .no-dark}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
AUPRC:
![](figures/yaib/transfer_learning_auprc.svg){fig-cap="Transfer Learning - Mortality AUPRC" width=100% .no-dark}

:::
::: -->

<!-- ## Conclusion
- True reproducibility in clinical ML requires more than just code release; it demands modular, flexible, and standardized pipelines that can adapt to the complexities of real-world data and research needs.
- YAIB: 96 GitHub stars; 44 citations; Adaption across community ^[[@shenDataAdditionDilemma2024a,@santosImprovingRepresentationLearning2025,@rockenschaubImpactMultiInstitutionDatasets2024,@fuentesPrivacyPreservingDatasetCombination2025]] -->

## {data-section-title="{{< var section_t3 >}}"}
<div style="position:absolute; inset:0; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; text-align:center; font-size:2.2em; font-weight:700;">
  ⚡ {{< var p3_label >}}
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
**Now that we've looked at structured open-access ICU data let's see how we can enable this  reproducible research across healthcare systems with more complex data and models.** 
:::
:::

# Framework for Foundational Health AI 🏗️ {data-section-title="{{< var section_t3 >}}"}

::: {.incremental}
​⚡ **{{< var p3_label >}}**: Training high-parameter AI models for heterogeneous data is difficult. 

::: {.fragment}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/neural_net_monochrome.svg){height=3em .light-only}

Event-based **Foundation Models (FMs)** show promise in Health AI.^[[@bommasaniOpportunitiesRisksFoundation2021;@steinbergLanguageModelsAre2021]]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/infrastructure.svg){height=3em .light-only}

Progress is hindered by **lack of reusable, scalable tooling.**^[[@mcdermottLackScienceMachine2025; @donohoDataScienceSingularity2024]]
:::
:::

<!-- **Example:** Mount Sinai Health System (MSHS) has 6.8 million patients
- **Before MEDS:** Data in OMOP format; every research project wrote custom extraction code (~3-6 weeks per project)
- **After MEDS:** Standardized ETL pipeline; research ready in days -->
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;" .fragment}
How can we **enable reproducible AI** research across healthcare systems?
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -0em; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; gap:0.6em;">
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_logo.svg" class="no-dark meds-logo" style="height:3em; vertical-align:middle;">
  <span class="meds-label">MEDS: Medical Event Data Standard ^[[[@vandewaterMedicalEventData2024] (Founding MEDS working Group)]{.my-work}]</span>
</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Unfortunately,health data is not always like the structured, clean, and well-labeled open-access ICU datasets that we just discussed. 
- Luckily, with the advent of Large Language Models, we now have high-parameter AI "foundation models", that are trained on partially structured large-scale EHR data, such as entire health systems, with the objective to predict the next token.
- However, with the increasing complexity of models and data, comparing results across settings becomes increasingly important, but more difficult. 
- Applying these high-parameter AI models to health data still lacks robust, standardized infrastructure, which is a significant barrier to reproducible research.
- This leads us to the question of how can we enable reproducible AI research across healthcare systems?
- Well, with a new data standard and ecosystem for health AI research, which we call the Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS).
- Donoho highlights the term of frictionless reproducibility for data science which highlights the growing need for robust infrastructure to support reproducible research across healthcare systems.
:::
:::
## Data Standards in Healthcare
<div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/meds/standards.png" width="50%" class="no-dark"> 
</div>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
  <a href="https://xkcd.com/927/" target="_blank" style="font-size: 0.5em; color:#6b6b6b; text-align: center;">Source: https://xkcd.com/927/</a>
</div>

::: { .incremental}
- OMOP ^[[@hripcsakObservationalHealthData2015]], PCORnet ^[[@collinsPCORnetTurningDream2014]], FHIR^[[@benderHL7FHIRAgile2013]], and i2b2^[[@murphyServingEnterpriseInformatics2010]].
- Have enabled **observational studies** and **health data exchange** ^[[@callahan2021using; @suchardComprehensiveComparativeEffectiveness2019]].
- But, they are not designed for **high-parameter AI research**.
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- I know that researchers are eager to create new data standards and frameworks, that might already exist as evident in this comic. 
- There are existing data standards in healthcare, such as OMOP, PCORnet, FHIR, and i2b2, have been instrumental in enabling observational studies and health data exchange. 
- They are Disease-specific cohorts and specific manual featurization of engineered features 
- But, they are generally not suitable to train over a general-purpose representation of the entire EHR
:::
:::

## The Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS)
::: {.callout-tip  appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;"}
::: {.incremental}
- **ML-First**: not observational research.
- **Flexible**: adapts to data source and research needs.
- **Event-Based**: longitudinal healthcare data.
- **Interoperable**: tools composable across settings.
:::
:::
<!-- ::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
<div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_schema_a.png" width="100%" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} -->
::: {.fragment}
<div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_schema_b.png" width="70%" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::
::: {.tiny-text}
**Adapted from McDermott et al. "MEDS: An Emerging Data Standard and Ecosystem for Health AI Research" Accepted to NEJM AI (2026), to appear in vol:3, iss:6 (2026).**
:::
<!-- :::
::: -->

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
We wanted to create a framework that is

1. ML-first: Optimized for ML tasks, not observational research.
2. **Flexible** - Works with OMOP, FHIR, open-access datasets, proprietary (private) health systems, and is quickly adaptable to new data sources.
3. **Event-Based** - Natural representation for longitudinal healthcare data
4. **Interoperable** - Tools compose together across datasets and institutions

- We created the Medical Event Data Standard (MEDS), a flexible, event-based data standard optimized for machine learning research in healthcare.

:::
:::


<!-- ```python
# Core MEDS Schema (v0.4.0)
from pyarrow.schema import Schema as PyArrowSchema
import pyarrow as pa
class DataSchema(PyArrowSchema):
    subject_id: Required(pa.int64(), nullable=False)
    time: Required(pa.timestamp("us"), nullable=True)
    code: Required(pa.string(), nullable=False)
    numeric_value: Optional(pa.float32())
    text_value: Optional(pa.large_string())
``` -->


## MEDS-DEV: Decentralized External Validation
- Reproducible benchmarking is **critical** for scientific progress.
- Sharing raw patient data across institutions is often not possible due to **privacy** concerns.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;" .fragment}
Define standardized **predicates** for tasks and have drop-in definitions for each dataset^[[[@mcdermottMEDSDecentralizedExtensible2024]{.my-work}][@xuACESAutomaticCohort2024a]].
:::

::: {.fragment}


::: {.columns}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div style="text-align: center;">
9 Datasets (1 private, 8 open-access)
![](figures/meds/map_meds_dev.svg){width=100% fig-align="center"}
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="25%" .fragment}
<div style="text-align: center;">
11 Tasks
![](icons/icu.svg){width=70% fig-align="center" .no-dark}
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="25%" .fragment}
<div style="text-align: center;">
3 Models^[@oufattoleMEDSTabAutomatedTabularization2024;@pangCEHRBERTIncorporatingTemporal2021; @hurGenHPFGeneralHealthcare2024a]

![](icons/neural_net_monochrome.svg){width=60% fig-align="center"}
</div>
:::

:::

<!-- {{< include resources/cohort_table_small.html >}} -->


::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- We can use MEDS to enable decentralized external validation across datasets and institutions.
- Reproducible benchmarking is **critical** for scientific progress, but sharing raw patient data across institutions is often not possible due to privacy concerns.
- The solution is to define standardized predicates for tasks and have drop-in definitions for each dataset, which allows for benchmarking across datasets without sharing raw patient data.
- MEDS-DEV is a collective effort that I expanded to 8 other datasets, including health systems open-access datasets
- Each site independently trains and evaluates models using the same standardized definitions, allowing for meaningful comparisons while respecting data privacy. 
- The configurations can be shared across sites, enabling researchers to identify factors that contribute to performance variability.
- 3 Complete Blood Count, 5 blood chemistry, 2 Vital Sign, Mortality, Readmission
- 3 models: MEDS-Tab, CEHR-BERT, GenHPF: a tabular transformer, a BERT-based model for EHR data, and a general healthcare foundation model.


:::
:::

---

<!-- ## Results depend heavily on dataset/task choice -->
<div style="margin-top: -0.5em;">
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1em;">
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_dev/model_legend.svg" width="35%" class="no-dark"     style="z-index:4; left:67%;"
>
</div>
<div style="position:relative; width:80%; margin:-1.5em auto 0; isolation:isolate;">

  <img
    src="figures/meds/meds_dev/model_comparison_auroc_task_grouped_samples.svg"
    width="100%"
    class="no-dark"
    style="position:relative; z-index:3; display:block;"
  >
  <img
    src="figures/meds/meds_dev/model_comparison_auprc_task_grouped_samples.svg"
    width="100%"
    class="no-dark"
    style="position:absolute; top:0; left:5%; z-index:1;"
  >
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
<div  style="text-align: center;  margin-top: -0.75em;">
Grouping by dataset and model:

  <img src="figures/meds/meds_dev/aggregated/auroc_by_dataset_samples.svg" width="100%" class="no-dark">
</div>

<div  style="text-align: center;  margin-top: -1.5em;">
Grouping by task and model:
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_dev/aggregated/auroc_by_task_samples.svg" width="100%" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple" style="font-size: 1.5em;" .fragment}
**No domination of one model** across datasets and tasks.
:::
</div>
:::
:::
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- If we then benchmark each task across sites and models, we get an enormous amount of results.
- Each point on the left represents a model trained and evaluated at a specific site, with the same task definition
- Some models incompatible with some datasets/tasks.
- The spread of points across sites for the same model and task highlights the variability in performance due to differences in data and implementation, underscoring the need for standardized benchmarking.
- The overall performance trends across models and tasks can be compared, but the variability emphasizes that results from one site may not generalize to others without careful validation.
- This highlights once again the importance of easy reproducibility and standardized benchmarking frameworks to understand what actually drives model performance across different settings.
:::
:::


## Foundation Models for Health Systems
  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
       style="position:absolute; top:28%; left:0%; width:100%; height:65%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>

  <div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
      style="position:absolute; top:28%; left:22%; width:100%; height:65%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
![](figures/meds/mount_sinai/benchmarking_mshs_new.svg){fig-cap="" width=100% .no-dark} ^[[@vandewaterScalingClinicalML2025; @vandewater2025flames]{.my-work}]
<div data-id="ms-logo-box" style="position:absolute; right:-3%; top:77%; width:12%; text-align:right;">
  <img src="images/logos/MS_logo.svg" style="width:50%;" class="no-dark">
</div>

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- So let's look at doing this on a health system level, where we use the entire Mount Sinai Health System dataset of 12 million patients across different medical centers around New York City. 
- This dataset is already in OMOP format, and we use the OMOP ETL I developed to convert it to MEDS. Leaving behind the legacy imported patients that have not correctly been imported into OMOP, we have 6.8 million patients and a total of 3.5 billion events in the dataset.
- The MEDS ecosystem then enables the use of different tools and of foundation model training for this dataset and the open-access datasets and other health systems
- Here, we will be looking at foundation model training and using the frozen embeddings for linear probing as a case study of how the MEDS ecosystem
- This can enable a wide range of applications, such as downstream clinical prediction systems to serve specialties in the health systems.

:::
:::

## What Can We Do With Foundation Models?
- Train SOTA **FMs**^[[@pangFoMoHClinicallyMeaningful2025]], CEHR-XGPT^[[@pangCEHRXGPTScalableMultiTask2025]] and MOTOR^[[@steinbergMOTORTimetoEventFoundation2023a]], on entire MSHS (5.5M patients).
- Embed a variety of **patient representations** until prediction time.
- **Linear probing**: train a **linear classifier** on top of frozen FM representations.
- Effect of **sample size** on performance:

<div data-id="ms-logo-box" style="position:absolute; right:-3%; top:77%; width:12%; text-align:right;">
  <img src="images/logos/MS_logo.svg" style="width:50%;" class="no-dark">
</div>
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -0.5em;">

![](figures/meds/linear_probing/tasks/linear_probing_legend.svg){width="100%" class="no-dark" }
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
![](figures/meds/linear_probing/aggregated/aggregated_line_roc_only.svg){width="100%" class="no-dark"}
</div>
</div>
<!-- <div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -2em;">
  <img src="figures/meds/linear_probing/aggregated/aggregated_line_roc_only.svg" width="50%" class="no-dark">
</div> -->
:::
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
→ For **small sample sizes**, FM representations can outperform existing machine learning models.

→ Cheaper than finetuning, and proxy for **quality of the foundation model representations** for downstream tasks.

→ FM-generated representations can provide a useful **starting point** for specialty AI-applications.

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- I trained state-of-the-art foundation models on the entire MSHS dataset, and then we used the frozen embeddings to train a linear classifier on top of the foundation model representations for a range of downstream clinical prediction tasks.
- The performance of the linear classifier on top of the frozen foundation model representations can be compared to traditional machine learning models trained on the same tasks, and this can help us understand the value of the foundation model representations for downstream tasks.
- Cheaper than finetuning, and can be used as a quick benchmark for the quality of the foundation model representations for downstream tasks.
- For lower sample set sizes, we see that the linear classifier on top of the foundation model representations can outperform traditional machine learning models, suggesting that the foundation model representations capture useful information for downstream tasks even with limited data.
- The performance gap decreases as we get more samples as can be expected, but there is still a slight boost compared to the baselines
- This suggests that the foundation model representations can provide a useful starting point for downstream tasks, even when finetuning is not feasible due to computational constraints or limited data.
Extra:

- This may reflect differences in pretraining strategies, as MOTOR is explicitly optimized for representation learning (e.g., time-to-event prediction), whereas CEHR-XGPT is trained using nexttoken prediction.
:::
:::

## MEDS: Community Adoption

<div style="text-align: center;">
  <img src="figures/meds/meds_adoption.png" width="100%" class="no-dark">
</div>
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="1"
      style="position:absolute; top:10%; left:63%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2"
      style="position:absolute; top:48%; left:0%; width:100%; height:100%; background:white; z-index:2;"></div>
::: {.tiny-text}
**Adapted from McDermott et al. "MEDS: An Emerging Data Standard and Ecosystem for Health AI Research" Accepted to NEJM AI (2026), to appear in vol:3, iss:6 (2026).**
:::
<!-- - Supported the development of SOTA models for EHR data ^[[@pangCEHRXGPTScalableMultiTask2025;@fallahpourEHRMambaGeneralizableScalable2024a; @rencFoundationModelElectronic2025]]. -->
<!-- - 21 institutions
- $>$ 27 academic papers and preprints
- 17 datasets
- 12 AI algorithms
- Reduction of lines of code up to 70% -->

<!-- ## My Thesis

We require: -->

<!-- ::: {.incremental}
1. **Transparent benchmarking frameworks 🧪**: to understand what actually drives model performance
2. **Prospective clinical studies 🛏️**: to improve quality and validate assumptions from retrospective research
3. **Standardized infrastructure 🏗️**: to enable collaborative, reproducible research at scale
:::  -->

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- So what is the impact of the MEDS ecosystem?
- It is used in at least 21 institutions, has supported the development of state-of-the-art models for EHR data, and has been used in over 27 academic papers and preprints across 17 datasets and 12 AI algorithms.
- It has also enabled a reduction of lines of code up to 70% for some projects
- I co-designed the schema and ecosystem and created 3 tools and 4 ETLs.


:::
:::
## {data-section-title="{{Discussion and Impact}}"}
<div style="position:absolute; inset:0; display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; text-align:center; font-size:2.2em; font-weight:700;">
  Discussion and Impact
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
**Let's summarize the key takeaways from my contributions:**
:::
:::

# Discussion and Impact



::: {.centered}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
<img src="figures/connecting_topics.svg" width="100%" class="no-dark">
</div>
:::
<div style="margin-top: -1em;">
::: {.columns .discussion-pillars}

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: { .pub-box .pub-box--pillar1 }
<!-- **{{< var section_t1 >}}** -->
<!-- → Alarm strategies key to **clinical utility**. -->

→ Continuous wearable data **nearly doubles AUPRC**.

→ Wearables provides **physiological insights**.

→ **Predictive surveillance** can be extended to the ward.
→ First author for **largest surgical wearable-enabled** prospective cohort.


:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment .pub-box .pub-box--pillar2 data-fragment-index="2"}
<!-- **{{< var section_t2 >}}** -->
→ Experimental setup often matters **more than model choice**.

→ Harmonizing data provides **external validation** and **fine-tunable models**.

→ Multi-center framework for **reproducible ML4H** (44 cit., GH 97 ⭐).

:::
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.fragment .pub-box .pub-box--pillar3 data-fragment-index="3"}
<!-- **{{< var section_t3 >}}** -->

<!-- - MEDS-DEV: easily benchmark tasks across different sites and tasks. -->
→ Co-founder of MEDS: enables **AI research across health systems**.

→ Created 4 Dataset, OMOP, FHIR ETLs and 3 tools.

<!-- → Benchmarking 9 datasets across 11 tasks. -->
→ MEDS-DEV: Scaled **cross-institutional** benchmarks.

→ **Adoption** across the community (87 agg. cit.; 113 GH followers).
:::
:::
:::
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="2" style="position:absolute; top:5.5%; left:23%; width:100%; height:44%; background:white;"></div>
<div class="fragment fade-out" data-fragment-index="3" style="position:absolute; top:7%; left:60%; width:100%; height:43%; background:white;"></div>

<!-- ::: {.fragment}
**Healthcare ML requires prospective data collection, experimental infrastructure, and institutional commitment to reproducibility.
**::: -->
<!-- ::: {.fragment .fade-in}
{{< include includes/statement.qmd >}}
::: -->
</div>
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Let's summarize the key takeaways from this thesis:
- For the first pillar, we found that continuous wearable data can nearly double the AUPRC for surgical site infection prediction, and that predictive surveillance can be used beyond the ICU into the ward. We also found that alarm strategies are key to clinical utility.
- For the second pillar, we found that the experimental setup often matters more than the choice of model, and we developed a multi-center framework for reproducible ML4H benchmarking that has been widely adopted in the community.
- For the third pillar, we developed the MEDS ecosystem, which enables reproducible AI research across health systems, and I, personally, created 4 dataset, OMOP, and FHIR ETLs and 3 tools, and benchmarked 9 datasets across 11 tasks.
- Overall, we see different scales of impact here, and we need to recognize that these scales all influence each other. For example, improvements in foundation modelling can inform the development of better early warning systems. And, simultaneously, collecting prospective data can inform the development of better multi-modal foundation models. 
:::
:::


# Limitations

::: {.fragment fragment-index=1}
::: {.card-row}
![](icons/people_split.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Amplification of existing racial, gender, and socioeconomic biases.**^[[@borgesdonascimentoBarriersFacilitatorsUtilizing2023]]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=2}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/check.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Need validation strategies and randomized trials before deployment.**^[[@wongExternalValidationWidely2021a; ]]
:::
:::

<!-- ::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/danger.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Need for oversight (e.g., insurance discrimination, surveillance).**^[[@gaffneyHealthCareUSA2025; @jangOopsInterdisciplinaryStories2025]]
:::
::: -->

::: {.fragment fragment-index=3}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/causal.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Need for causal approaches to improve theoretical validity.** ^[[@joshiAIInterventionImproving2025b]]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment fragment-index=4}
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/neural_net_monochrome.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**(Large) AI models have environmental impact and are "black boxes".**^[[@hosseiniSocialenvironmentalImpactPerspective2025]]
:::
:::
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Models may amplify existing (historical) racial, gender, and socioeconomic biases if not carefully validated because they are trained on historical data that reflects these biases.
- Validation strategies and randomized controlled trials are needed to demonstrate clinical utility and safety before deployment. We might not be able to take shortcuts here, even though rcts are expensive and time-consuming, because of the potential for harm if models are deployed without proper validation.
- Working backward from the end goal of positively impacting clinically relevant outcomes using AI, leads to considerations of causality in model development and validation, and subsequently a better development pipeline. Healthcare AI should be “actionable,” and the change in actions induced by AI should improve outcomes. Quantifying the effect of changes in actions on outcomes is causal inference. 
- Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) emulation / Target Trials	Algorithmically emulates randomized studies from observational data	Drug efficacy estimation
- Propensity Score Matching	Balances treatment/control groups on observed confounders	Comparing surgical vs. medical interventions 
- (Large) AI models can have sizeable environmental impact. We see this in the training of large language models, and this is something that we need to be mindful of as we develop and deploy AI models in healthcare. We should strive to develop simpler models where possible as this can even lead to an improved physiological understanding as we saw in the first contribution. 
:::
:::




# Future Research Directions

::: {.fragment }
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/multi_modality.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Advances in multi-modal health Foundation Models.**
:::
:::
::: {.fragment }
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/multiple_hospital.svg){height=3em .light-only} 

**Generalizability of Foundation Models in health.**^[[@burkhartFoundationModelsElectronic2025a;@futomaMythGeneralisabilityClinical2020a; @vancalsterThereNoSuch2023]]

:::
:::

::: {.fragment }
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/graph.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Scaling up: datasets, model size, benchmarking.**^[[@waxlerGenerativeMedicalEvent2025; @zhangExploringScalingLaws2025; @huangOverTokenizedTransformerVocabulary2025]]
:::
:::

::: {.fragment }
::: {.card-row .card-row--stack}
![](icons/clinician.svg){height=3em .light-only}

**Predictive surveillance and transferable patient representations.**^[[@kernCareFragmentationCare2024]] 
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- Multi-modal: Clinical Notes, Pathology, Radiology, Genomics, Wearables, etc. I am currently working on expanding my FMs to pathology as this is widely available in MSHS.
- Health data is different from text used in LLMs, because different health systems have different data generating processes and different patient populations. So, understanding the generalizability of foundation models in health is an important area of research.
- Scaling up: how does performance improve with more data, compute, and model size? Understanding scaling laws in health AI can inform the development of more efficient and effective models.
- Predictive surveillance: We can schedule mammograms in advance if a patient is at high risk of developing breast cancer.
- Finally, we can develop representations that combat fragmentation across different healthcare providers (GP and specialists), which can lead to better continuity of care and better patient outcomes.

:::
:::

---



::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
Coming back to the those 84 patients that we started with, we can see that this could just be the beginning of AI in healthcare. By closing the reproducibility gap, we can enable scientist to build upon each others work rather than each starting from scratch. This should lead to  prolonged patient lives through trustworthy and equitable healthcare AI for the many.
<!-- 
I hope together, we can continue to create more reproducible research and build better tools to enable the development of better AI models for healthcare, which can ultimately lead to better patient outcomes and more saved lives. -->


:::
:::
<!--
## United Nations WHO Digital Health Objectives

::: {.centered}
![](figures/motivation/WHO_logo.svg){fig-cap="World Health Organization" height=5em .no-dark}
:::

::: {.bigger-text}

::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=1}

::: {.fragment .highlight-green fragment-index=5}
   - Promote global collaboration & advance the transfer of knowledge on digital health
:::
::: 
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=2}
   - Advance the implementation of national digital health strategies
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=3}
   - Strengthen governance for digital health at global, regional and national levels
:::
::: {.fragment .fade-in fragment-index=4}
   - Advocate people-centered health systems that are enabled by digital health
:::

:::
::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
In my thesis we focus on 1 in particularly while respecting and complementing the other objectives; of which the latter 3 and 4 are most important.
- https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-T/Workshops-and-Seminars/2024/0509/Documents/Carl%20LEITNER.pdf 
::: -->

```{=html}
<style>
.reveal .slides section:has(.grid9){overflow:visible!important}
.reveal .slides section:has(#refs){overflow:hidden!important}
.reveal .slides section:has(#refs) #refs{max-height:100%;overflow-y:auto}
.grid9 { position:absolute; top:0; left:0; width:100%; height:100%; display:grid; grid-template-columns:1fr 1fr 1fr; grid-template-rows:1fr 1fr 1fr; gap:0; pointer-events:none; }
.grid9 div { display:flex; align-items:stretch; justify-content:center; min-height:0; overflow:visible; }
.grid9 img { width:100%; height:100%; object-fit:contain; transform:scale(1.25, 1.10); transform-origin:center; display:block; }
</style>

  <div class="fragment" data-fragment-index="1"><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>
  <div><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>
  <div class="fragment" data-fragment-index="1"><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>

  <div class="fragment" data-fragment-index="1"><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>
  <div class="fragment" data-fragment-index="1"><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>
  <div class="fragment" data-fragment-index="1"><img src="resources/people_slide.svg" alt="People slide"></div>
</div>
```

# Acknowledgements {data-background-image="figures/backgrounds/cover_image_thesis_wide.png" data-background-size="cover" data-background-position="center" data-background-opacity="0.15"}
**All friends, colleagues, mentors, collaborators, reviewers, and family who supported me.**

::: {.small-text}
::: {.columns}

- Jason Fries (Stanford)

- Mykola Pechenizkiy (TU Eindhoven)
:::
:::
:::

::: {.columns}
<!-- <div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
</div> -->
::: {.column width="33%"}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
![](figures/slides_link_trans.svg){width=80% .no-dark .center}
</div>

</div>
:::

![](figures/thesis_defense_png.png){width=100% .no-dark .center}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
  <a href="https://xkcd.com/1403/" target="_blank" style="font-size: 0.5em; text-align: center;">Source: https://xkcd.com/1403/</a>
</div>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1em;">
![](images/logos/logos_defense.svg){width=100% .no-dark}
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.5em;">
![](figures/thesis_link_trans.svg){width=80% .no-dark .center}
</div>
<div style="text-align: center; margin-top: -1.0em;">

<a href="https://www.rpvandewater.com/assets/documents/van_de_Water_dissertation_thesis_22_jan.pdf" target="_blank">
Read the thesis.</a>
</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
::: {style="font-size:0.7em"}
- With that, I want to thank all the people who supported me during this journey and all people that are present here today.
:::
:::
<!-- The field of machine learning for healthcare stands at an inflection point. We have:
- ✓ Demonstrated predictive capability (models work in controlled settings)
- ✓ Shown deployment is feasible (CASSANDRA, clinical partnerships)
- ✓ Built reusable infrastructure (MEDS ecosystem)

**What Remains:**
- Translating research into routine clinical practice (workflow integration)
- Ensuring equitable deployment across diverse healthcare systems
- Maintaining humility about what ML can and cannot do in healthcare --> 



<!-- ## Thank You -->

<!-- *Key Takeaways:*
1. Reproducibility framework (YAIB) reveals that experimental setup often matters more than model choice
2. Prospective validation (CASSANDRA) shows what works in reality, not just in retrospective analysis
3. Standardized data format (MEDS) enables community collaboration and faster scientific progress -->

<!-- *Broader Vision:*
Healthcare ML can improve outcomes when built on solid infrastructure, rigorous validation, and commitment to reproducibility. -->


## References {data-background-image="figures/backgrounds/cover_image_thesis_wide.png" data-background-size="cover" data-background-position="center" data-background-opacity="0.15"}

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< include extra_slides/motivation.qmd >}}
{{< include extra_slides/cassandra.qmd >}}
{{< include extra_slides/yaib.qmd >}}
{{< include extra_slides/meds.qmd >}}


<script>
{{< include fade_in_footnotes.js >}}
</script>
<script>
{{< include section_indicator.js >}}
</script>
<!-- <script>
(function () {
  const FADE_MS = 500;

  function getFootnoteAside(slide) {
    if (!slide) return null;
    const asides = slide.querySelectorAll(':scope > aside');
    for (const aside of asides) {
      if (aside.querySelector(':scope > ol.aside-footnotes')) return aside;
    }
    return null;
  }

  function getVisibleFootnoteNumbers(slide) {
    const nums = new Set();
    const sups = slide.querySelectorAll('sup');

    sups.forEach((sup) => {
      const frag = sup.closest('.fragment');
      const fragmentVisible =
        !frag ||
        frag.classList.contains('visible') ||
        frag.classList.contains('current-fragment');

      if (!fragmentVisible) return;

      const n = Number.parseInt((sup.textContent || '').trim(), 10);
      if (Number.isFinite(n)) nums.add(n);
    });

    return nums;
  }

  function ensureFadeInit(el) {
    if (!el || el.dataset.fnInit === '1') return;
    el.dataset.fnInit = '1';
    el.style.transition = 'opacity ' + FADE_MS + 'ms ease';
    el.style.opacity = '0';
    el.style.display = 'none';
  }

  function setFadeState(el, show) {
    if (!el) return;
    ensureFadeInit(el);

    const wasShown = el.dataset.fnShown === '1';
    if (show === wasShown) return;
    el.dataset.fnShown = show ? '1' : '0';

    if (show) {
      el.style.display = '';
      el.style.opacity = '0';
      requestAnimationFrame(() => {
        if (el.dataset.fnShown === '1') {
          el.style.opacity = '1';
        }
      });
    } else {
      el.style.opacity = '0';
      window.setTimeout(() => {
        if (el.dataset.fnShown !== '1') {
          el.style.display = 'none';
        }
      }, FADE_MS);
    }
  }

  function syncSlideFootnotes(slide) {
    const footAside = getFootnoteAside(slide);
    if (!footAside) return;

    const list = footAside.querySelector(':scope > ol.aside-footnotes');
    if (!list) return;

    const visibleNums = getVisibleFootnoteNumbers(slide);
    let anyShown = false;

    Array.from(list.children).forEach((li, idx) => {
      const liNum = idx + 1;
      const show = visibleNums.has(liNum);

      setFadeState(li, show);
      if (show) anyShown = true;
    });

    setFadeState(footAside, anyShown);
  }

  function refresh(evt) {
    const slide =
      (evt && evt.currentSlide) ||
      (window.Reveal && Reveal.getCurrentSlide && Reveal.getCurrentSlide());

    if (slide) syncSlideFootnotes(slide);
  }

  function init() {
    if (!window.Reveal) return;
    Reveal.on('ready', refresh);
    Reveal.on('slidechanged', refresh);
    Reveal.on('fragmentshown', refresh);
    Reveal.on('fragmenthidden', refresh);
    refresh();
  }

  if (document.readyState === 'loading') {
    document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
  } else {
    init();
  }
})();
</script> -->
<!-- {{< include extra_slides/publications.qmd >}} -->
